title: Confidence-Dynamic Heterogeneous Reasoning: Adaptive Strategy Selection via Uncertainty Trajectories
description: We propose CDHR, a training-free framework that uses confidence dynamics (velocity and variance of uncertainty trajectories) to adaptively select between heterogeneous reasoning strategies at the step level. Unlike prior work that uses confidence only for continuation decisions or requires complex Meta-Agent architectures, CDHR treats confidence trajectories as direct signals for reasoning modality selection, enabling seamless transitions between linear deduction, analogical transfer, decomposition, and verification strategies without learned components.
motivation: Current LLM reasoning methods apply homogeneous strategies throughout problem-solving, failing to address the heterogeneous nature of complex reasoning. While Chain of Mindset (CoM) enables dynamic mindset switching, it requires a complex Meta-Agent architecture with Context Gates and multiple mindset modules. CoRefine uses confidence-guided adaptation but requires training a learned controller and focuses on refinement rather than heterogeneous strategy selection. There is a gap for a lightweight, training-free method that uses internal signals to directly guide strategy selection without auxiliary models or complex architectures.
proposed_approach: CDHR continuously monitors confidence trajectories using token entropy and self-consistency. It computes confidence velocity (rate of change) and variance (stability) at each reasoning step. Based on these dynamics: (1) Progressing trajectories (positive velocity, low variance) continue with linear deduction; (2) Stagnant trajectories (near-zero velocity) trigger analogical transfer with relevance verification and fallback; (3) Oscillating trajectories (high variance) activate decompositional analysis; (4) Declining trajectories (negative velocity) initiate verification and backtrack. Strategy transitions use structured prompts with state passing, eliminating the need for Meta-Agents or Context Gates. Thresholds are empirically justified through grid search validation.
theoretical_foundation: CDHR is grounded in information theory: confidence is interpreted as entropy reduction in the model's predictive distribution, velocity corresponds to the rate of information gain (Data Processing Inequality), and variance indicates epistemic uncertainty. These theoretical links justify the mapping from confidence dynamics to reasoning states.

related_work.title: Chain of Mindset: Reasoning with Adaptive Cognitive Modes
related_work.difference: CoM uses a Meta-Agent and Context Gate for mindset selection, requiring complex multi-component architecture. CDHR eliminates the Meta-Agent by using confidence dynamics as the direct selection signal, reducing latency and system complexity.

related_work.title: CoRefine: Confidence-Guided Self-Refinement for Adaptive Test-Time Compute
related_work.difference: CoRefine trains a learned Conv1D controller (211K parameters) for HALT/RETHINK/ALTERNATIVE decisions. CDHR is fully training-free and uses confidence dynamics for heterogeneous strategy selection rather than iterative refinement.

related_work.title: MixReasoning: Switching Modes to Think
related_work.difference: MixReasoning uses trained LoRA adapters to switch between detailed and concise reasoning modes. CDHR is training-free and switches between heterogeneous reasoning modalities (deduction, analogy, decomposition) rather than just reasoning depth.

related_work.title: ReBalance: Efficient Reasoning with Balanced Thinking
related_work.difference: ReBalance uses confidence signals for hidden state steering to balance over/under-thinking, requiring white-box access. CDHR works with black-box APIs and switches between reasoning strategies rather than steering within a single mode.

related_work.title: Confidence-Guided Early Stopping for Efficient and Accurate Self-Consistency (CGES)
related_work.difference: CGES uses confidence for early stopping in parallel sampling. CDHR uses confidence dynamics for step-level strategy selection within a single reasoning chain, not for stopping decisions.

hypothesis: A training-free framework that monitors confidence trajectories and adaptively switches between heterogeneous reasoning strategies based on confidence dynamics (velocity and variance) will achieve better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs than static strategies or confidence-based halting alone, without requiring learned components or Meta-Agent architectures.

success_criteria.primary: CDHR achieves accuracy within 2% of Chain of Mindset with ≥30% lower latency, OR outperforms standard CoT by ≥5% with ≤20% token overhead.
secondary: Strategy selection demonstrates meaningful adaptation (entropy of strategy distribution > 0.5 bits)
Confidence dynamics predict correctness with precision ≥80%
Method generalizes across model families without re-tuning thresholds
Analogical retrieval relevance rate ≥70% (when fallback is not triggered)
failure_modes: Confidence dynamics not reliably signaling reasoning state (would require richer features)
Strategy transitions introducing noise (would require better state passing mechanisms)
Thresholds not generalizing across problem types (would require adaptive threshold selection)
Analogical retrieval consistently failing (would require better retrieval index)

ablations: Confidence signal ablation (token vs consistency vs combined)
Dynamics features ablation (velocity vs variance vs combined)
Beta sensitivity analysis for composite confidence weight

pre_validation.description: Validate core confidence dynamics assumptions on held-out validation set (100 GSM8K problems)
checks: Progressing trajectories correlate with correct steps (precision ≥75%)
Oscillating trajectories occur 2x more on hard problems

computational_requirements.expected_runtime: ~4 hours for full evaluation (2 hours validation + threshold selection, 2 hours benchmark)
computational_requirements.framework: Python with vLLM for efficient inference

key_innovations: Training-free confidence dynamics for heterogeneous strategy selection
Information-theoretic justification linking confidence velocity to information gain rate
Relevance-verified analogical retrieval with fallback mechanism
Safeguards for unreliable confidence estimation (OOD detection, calibration check)
Sensitivity analysis framework for key hyperparameters (β, θ_v, θ_σ)
novelty_claim: CDHR is the first method to use confidence dynamics (velocity and acceleration of uncertainty trajectories) as a direct signal for heterogeneous reasoning strategy selection. Unlike CoM which requires a Meta-Agent and Context Gate, or CoRefine which requires training a controller, CDHR enables step-level strategy switching in a purely training-free, black-box manner using only internal confidence signals, with theoretical grounding in information theory and empirical threshold validation.
